sources.notes: Read the full statement, the initially comfortable condition 2*a_i >= a_{i+1}, all bounds, and the per-query at-most-k objective.

Official Hello 2025 editorial. Its prose was not rendered, but both included accepted-style implementations were inspected: the primary budget-event sweep with one multiset per propagation depth, and the alternative target-interval envelope with parallel binary search.
Cross-checked the piecewise minimum-cost function, the O(log V) propagated suffix, budget-axis breakpoints, heap/multiset sweep, and O(n log V log n + q log^2 V) implementation.

Cross-checked the alternative derivation by fixed affected length: each endpoint supplies a target-height interval and suffix-sum weight, then sorted midpoint queries scan the piecewise maxima in parallel.

Accepted C++23 submission by NanguX inspected directly (1640 ms, 147588 KB). It independently confirms 31 propagation levels, prefix/suffix sums, sorted queries, per-depth multisets, and the half-open endpoint K_m+1 that avoids breakpoint tie errors.

Independently proved the minimal propagated-suffix construction and monotone budget breakpoints. A local read-only randomized checker compared the event-sweep predicate with direct min_i sum_d max(0, ceil(x/2^d)-a_{i-d}) on 50,000 random comfortable arrays/query thresholds; every case matched.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate requested by the workflow, but the solution page returned an internal error during this review and was not used as evidence.

annotation.summary: For each pizza budget k, maximize the final array maximum after only increasing entries while preserving 2*b_i >= b_{i+1}.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 10^4; 1 <= n,q <= 5*10^4 per test; sums of n and q are each at most 5*10^4; 1 <= a_i,k <= 10^9; the input array already satisfies 2*a_i >= a_{i+1}. Any answer is at most max(a)+k <= 2*10^9, so only 31 halving levels matter.
annotation.core_idea: Fix a threshold x and a position r that will reach x. The cheapest valid construction leaves everything to the right unchanged and, moving left, sets b_{r-d}=max(a_{r-d},ceil(x/2^d)); raising unrelated positions cannot help. Initial comfort implies the positive deficits form one contiguous suffix ending at r. If its active length is m, define H_m(x)=sum_{d=0}^{m-1} ceil(x/2^d) and S_{r,m}=sum_{p=r-m+1}^{r} a_p, so its cost is H_m(x)-S_{r,m}. In the inverse view for a fixed budget, endpoint r uses length m between consecutive breakpoints. With K_{r,0}=0 and, when r-m exists, K_{r,m}=H_m(2^m*a_{r-m})-S_{r,m}=(2^(m+1)-2)*a_{r-m}-S_{r,m}, length m is active for K_{r,m-1} <= k <= K_{r,m}; the last breakpoint is infinity. Moreover K_{r,m}-K_{r,m-1}=(2^m-1)*(2*a_{r-m}-a_{r-m+1}) >= 0, so these are ordered intervals. Sweep all interval endpoints and query budgets in increasing k. For every m maintain the maximum active S_{r,m}. At a query k, threshold x is feasible exactly when some m satisfies H_m(x)-maxS_m(k) <= k. This predicate is monotone in x, so binary-search the answer.
annotation.complexity: Let L=31=O(log V). The primary solution uses O(n*L*log(n*L) + n*L*log n + q*L*log V) time, conventionally written O(n log V log n + q log^2 V), and O(n log V) memory.
tricks: invert the query: characterize the affected suffix length by budget breakpoints rather than by the binary-searched height
separate each cost into the universal H_m(x) minus an endpoint-dependent suffix sum
use initial comfort to prove both contiguous deficit propagation and monotone breakpoints
at an equal breakpoint, either process insertion before query before deletion for closed intervals or use a half-open end K_m+1 consistently
use signed 64-bit arithmetic for geometric coefficients, suffix sums, costs, and infinity sentinels
review_risks: The official editorial prose rendered only as 'Tutorial is loading'; correctness was reconstructed from both official implementations, then cross-checked against two independent explanations, an accepted submission, an algebraic proof, and 50,000 randomized predicate comparisons.
The main implementation risk is an off-by-one at K_m. Mixing the official closed-interval event order with the accepted code's half-open K_m+1 convention produces one-budget errors.

Implementations must keep the propagation-depth convention consistent: m is a count of affected positions, while several sources store it zero-based. Using 31 levels and 1LL shifts is the conservative choice for the 2*10^9 answer bound.
The Codeforces official tag dp was not retained: the reviewed solutions use a closed-form propagated-suffix cost plus offline envelopes, not a dynamic-programming state transition.
The proposed algorithm/search/parallel-binary-search tag is new and requires main-agent taxonomy approval plus WebUI translation review before application.

solution_variants.name: budget-sweep-per-query-binary-search
solution_variants.summary: For every endpoint r and active length m, emit the budget interval between consecutive propagation breakpoints with weight S_{r,m}. Sweep these events together with all query budgets, maintaining a maximum multiset of active suffix sums for each m. At each query, binary-search x and test min_m(H_m(x)-maxS_m)<=k in O(L).
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log V log n + q log^2 V) time and O(n log V) memory.

solution_variants.name: target-envelope-parallel-binary-search
solution_variants.summary: For each length m, derive every endpoint's target-height interval on which its m-term formula is valid, sweep interval endpoints to build the piecewise maximum suffix sum maxS_m(x), and run all query answer searches in synchronized rounds. Sorting current midpoints lets one pointer per m scan the precomputed change lists and evaluate every feasibility check without an extra logarithmic lookup.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log V log n + (n+q) log^2 V + q log V log q) time in the official implementation and O(n log V) memory.

tags.evidence: For each budget k, feasibility of reaching threshold x is monotone because every H_m(x) is nondecreasing; the primary solution binary-searches the largest feasible x.
All O(n log V) budget-interval starts and ends are sorted with query budgets, and the active maximum suffix sum for each propagation length is maintained during one ordered sweep.
Initial comfort makes deficits a contiguous left-propagating suffix and gives K_m-K_{m-1}=(2^m-1)(2a_{r-m}-a_{r-m+1})>=0, which is what turns each endpoint/length into one budget interval.
Queries are reordered by budget and answered while sharing the same preprocessed event sweep; no online per-query rebuild is performed.
The primary implementation needs duplicate-safe insertion/deletion of active suffix sums and retrieval of the current maximum independently for each propagation length.

tags.description: Run many monotone binary searches in synchronized rounds, ordering their current midpoints so shared offline state or monotone scans can answer all feasibility checks in a batch; use when this batching, not merely independent binary search, removes a logarithmic factor.
tags.created_reason: The existing binary-search-on-answer tag covers independent threshold searches but does not identify the reusable synchronized multi-query technique. The official alternative for CF2057F explicitly uses parallel binary search to share scans of the target-height envelopes and reduce an extra logarithmic factor. Candidate requires main-agent taxonomy and WebUI-translation review.
tags.evidence: The alternative advances all q answer searches round by round, orders the current midpoints, and evaluates them together against shared piecewise max-suffix-sum envelopes.
Within each parallel-binary-search round, current midpoints are processed in sorted order while one monotone pointer per propagation length advances through its envelope breakpoints.
